title: Best Parts Washers of 2026: Solvent Compatibility for Your Shop
excerpt: Which parts washers actually hold up to daily use, match your solvent of choice, and fit your shop size without surprise leaks or rust.
author: Editorial Team
metaDescription: Which parts washers actually hold up to daily use, match your solvent of choice, and fit your shop size without surprise leaks or rust.
intro:
A parts washer seems simple: a metal basin, a pump, some solvent. But the moment you pour in mineral spirits only to have the pump fail, or watch the paint peel off the interior, you realize that not all washers are built the same.

You need a unit that fits your shop — maybe a portable bench‑top for small carbs, or a deep 40‑gallon tank for engine blocks. And you need to know which models are limited to water‑based degreasers so you don't ruin the pump on day one. The right washer turns hours of scrubbing into a quick cycle.

The picks below avoid the most common traps: leaky connections, shipping damage from heavy steel, and pumps that quit after a week. Each one earns its place by matching a specific shop reality.

products.title: Red Sun Parts Washer 20 Gallon Cabinet Electric Solvent Pump Portable Automotive Parts Cleaner with Shelf Tank for Wheel Bearings, Gears, and carburetors
products.label: Red Sun 20-Gallon Parts Washer
products.oneLiner: 20‑gallon steel basin with 5.25 GPM pump and flow‑through brush for home auto repair.
products.usageNote: 💡 Tip: Pair this washer with a premium water-based degreaser; the right chemistry delivers cleaning power comparable to mineral spirits without harming the pump.
pros: Strong 5.25 GPM flow clears grease and grime faster than many units in this price tier.
Includes flow-through brush and removable parts basket, eliminating typical accessory add-ons. — 6 mentions, 83.3% positive
Alloy steel construction with anti-rust coating feels sturdy for home shop use. — 14 mentions, 78.6% positive
Tool-free assembly and intuitive controls get you cleaning in under 30 minutes. — 11 mentions, 90.9% positive
20-gallon capacity accommodates cylinder heads, manifolds, and other mid-size components.
cons: Interior paint may peel and surface rust can form when using certain alkaline water-based cleaners. — 10 mentions, 50% negative — paint peeling and surface rust reported
Pump longevity varies; some units may need re-priming or stop spinning after limited use. — 16 mentions, 37.5% negative — occasional pump glitches or total failure
Compatible exclusively with water-based solvents; mineral spirits will damage the pump. — 6 mentions, 50% negative — disappointment over solvent restriction
products.verdict: For the home mechanic who sticks to water-based degreasers and doesn't run the unit daily, the Red Sun's high flow rate, included accessories, and generous capacity make it a compelling mid-range choice — but it isn't built for petroleum solvents or around-the-clock shop duty.
products.review:
The Red Sun 20-Gallon Parts Washer hits the garage with a 5.25 GPM pump that pushes solvent through a flow-through brush and a gooseneck spigot — a combination normally reserved for pricier units. The 20-gallon tank holds enough capacity for cylinder heads, motorcycle engine cases, and a full set of brake parts, while the alloy steel body and anti-rust coating keep the unit stable on a workbench. From the first unboxing, the complete kit signals minimal add-on spending: the removable basket, brush, and drain plug are all in the box.

The pump's water-resistant seals enforce a firm compatibility line: petroleum-based solvents will degrade the internals and void the warranty. Stick with water-based degreasers, and the system runs smoothly, with a flow rate that slices through baked-on grease and carbon without constant nozzle adjustment. Build quality feels sturdy for the mid-range price, though the interior paint can react to highly alkaline cleaners, leading to peeling and surface rust over time. Pumps have shown variable longevity; some units need re-priming or stop spinning after a few weekends, though many run without issue.

This washer fits squarely in the home garage of a weekend mechanic who tackles dirt bikes, lawn tractor engines, and general automotive parts. The fixed cabinet design and 20-gallon volume mean it stays put on a bench or stand — portability is not its strength. If you plan to use the machine every day in a commercial shop or insist on mineral spirits, the Red Sun will disappoint; the pump's reliability curve is better matched to occasional use, and the solvent restriction is non-negotiable. For the right user, the all-in-one value proposition cuts through the typical part-by-part purchase of a brush, basket, and gooseneck separately.

The flow-through brush deserves particular attention. Instead of a standard stiff brush, Red Sun integrates a solvent line directly into the handle, allowing continuous fresh fluid to hit the part while scrubbing. This design dramatically reduces the time spent dipping back into the tank and keeps hands out of the solvent. Along with the high GPM, it turns tough carbon deposits into a quick rinse-and-scrub cycle. The pump auto-shutoff mechanism can trip if the motor overheats, but resetting it takes only a few seconds — a minor quirk in an otherwise smooth experience.

💡 💡 Tip: Pair this washer with a premium water-based degreaser; the right chemistry delivers cleaning power comparable to mineral spirits without harming the pump.

products.label: JEGS 5-Gallon Portable Parts Washer
products.oneLiner: Steel 5‑gallon portable with 3.96 GPM pump, small enough for a roll cart.
products.usageNote: 💡 Tip: Attach an inline brush or flow-control valve to keep spray inside the tub — the pump pressure is generous.
pros: Cleans gears, bearings, and small transmission parts thoroughly. — 36 mentions, 83.3% positive
Cost-effective for a portable steel washer. — 18 mentions, 88.9% positive

cons: High spray pressure may splash solvent over the basin edge without a brush attachment. — 6 pump pressure mentions, 16.7% negative

products.verdict: For small parts and grab-and-go portability, this steel washer is a smart buy; just plan to add a brush and set aside cash for a pump down the road if you run it daily.
products.review:
The 3.5-gallon solvent capacity handles grimy chainsaw parts or a carburetor teardown without pausing to refill. The powder-coated steel tub resists dents and chemical attack better than plastic alternatives, and the compact shape leaves room on a standard roll cart for brushes and scrapers.

At 17 pounds with a tidy footprint, this JEGS unit redefines a parts washer as a grab-and-go tool rather than a stationary tub. Where larger 20-gallon washers demand permanent floor space, this 5-gallon steel model slides onto a roll cart, making it easy to clean motorcycle parts, chainsaw components, or transmission gears wherever the work happens.

DIYers who tinker with small engines, carburetors, or bicycle drivetrains will appreciate the portability and sturdy build. For heavy commercial use or daily cleaning with mineral spirits, the pump may not stay reliable over the long haul — occasional weekend service is its sweet spot. The spray is forceful enough to splash outside the basin, but adding an inexpensive brush attachment solves that entirely.

💡 💡 Tip: Attach an inline brush or flow-control valve to keep spray inside the tub — the pump pressure is generous.

products.label: Eastwood 40-Gal Washer
products.oneLiner: Heavy‑duty 40‑gallon tank with 5.3 GPM pump and lifetime tech support for big components.
products.usageNote: 💡 Tip: To prolong the brush hose, drain it after each use and hang it straight — solvent residue inside the hose accelerates stiffening if left coiled.
pros: Heavy-duty steel construction and pump that runs consistently without bogging down. — 13 quality mentions, 92.3% positive; 8 functionality mentions, 100% positive
Deep 40-gallon tank swallows engine blocks, heads, and other large assemblies. — Multiple top reviews praise 'great tank, hvy duty'
5.3 GPM pump circulates solvent fast enough to clear heavy grease with the flow-through brush.
Lifetime technical support from Eastwood backs the purchase indefinitely.
cons: Flexible brush-hose attachment can stiffen and lose pliability after repeated solvent exposure. — 1 top review highlights stiffening hose — limited data but consistent with material choice

products.verdict: Big-engine builders and professional shops that can dedicate floor space to a permanent tank will appreciate the Eastwood's cavernous basin and lifetime support. For everyone else, the top pick delivers comparable cleaning flow with far more flexibility.
products.review:
Where the top pick balances generous capacity with mobility, the Eastwood 40-Gallon Washer leans fully into shop-grade volume. Its tall basin can submerge a V8 engine block or an entire cylinder head, something a 20-gallon tank simply cannot do. A 5.3 GPM pump and heavy-duty steel build set it apart from lighter-duty alternatives, making it a fixture-level tool rather than a portable helper.

The pump circulates solvent consistently even when the tank is loaded with parts, and the flow-through brush delivers a strong stream to wash away caked-on grease. The powder-coated steel tank handles petroleum-based solvents that would degrade plastic competitors. Because Eastwood provides lifetime technical support, a shop that depends on the washer daily has a direct line for troubleshooting — a rarity in this category.

This washer is precisely for professional mechanics and restoration shops where large-part cleaning is routine. The tradeoff is a permanent footprint: at 92 pounds and nearly three feet wide, it demands a dedicated bay — moving it around the shop is impractical. The included flexible brush hose adds convenience initially, but over time the hose material can stiffen, making it less cooperative during fine work. Home hobbyists who work on smaller assemblies or need to store the unit after use will find the top pick’s lower weight and more versatile form a better match.

💡 💡 Tip: To prolong the brush hose, drain it after each use and hang it straight — solvent residue inside the hose accelerates stiffening if left coiled.

products.title: Parts Washer 3.5 Gallon Capacity Tank Cabinet Portable Automotive Parts Cleaner for Wheel Bearings, Gears, and carburetors (Blue)…
products.label: Red Sun 3.5-Gal Washer
products.oneLiner: Compact 3.5‑gallon washer with 4 GPM pump and flow‑through brush for small parts.
products.usageNote: 💡 Tip: Place a shallow drip tray under the gooseneck and snug the fittings with thread tape to keep the workbench dry.
pros: Effectively strips old grime from chainsaw parts and differential internals — 33 effectiveness mentions, 81.8% positive
Excellent value for a compact bench-top unit with included brush — 15 value mentions, 86.7% positive
Lightweight and easy to move, sets up with minimal assembly — 10 ease-of-use mentions, 90% positive; 14.3 lbs
cons: The 4 GPM stream can feel underpowered on especially stubborn, baked-on deposits — 8 pump power mentions, 50% negative — some users wanted more pressure
products.verdict: For the bench-top cleaner of small, intricate parts where portability and a built-in brush matter, this Red Sun washer provides strong value—just plan for a drip tray beneath the gooseneck.
products.review:
For mechanics working on small components at a crowded bench, the Red Sun 3.5-Gal Washer delivers 4 GPM of solvent flow through a brush-tipped nozzle in a 14.3-lb steel cabinet that tucks away easily.

The flow-through brush scrubs while pushing fresh cleaner, cutting through residue on carburetors, chainsaw clutches, and brake brackets without needing a separate scrub step. Setup is tool-free aside from attaching the hose, and the low weight lets you shift it from shelf to bench as needed.

It fits the rhythm of bicycle mechanics, small-engine tinkerers, and anyone whose parts cleaning involves hand-sized items rather than soaked engine blocks. The pump is strictly compatible with water-based solvents, so mineral spirits will damage the seals. The gooseneck base and lower hose connections can weep fluid—plan on a shallow containment tray and a quick snug of the fittings with thread tape to keep the work area dry. For thick, baked-on sludge there are stronger pumps, but for light-to-moderate grime it gets the job done without dominating a workbench.

💡 💡 Tip: Place a shallow drip tray under the gooseneck and snug the fittings with thread tape to keep the workbench dry.

products.review:
Unlike the top pick which requires water-based cleaners, this 20-gallon steel tank handles petroleum solvents — a deciding factor for mechanics who rely on mineral spirits to cut heavy grease. The 12-gallon solvent capacity and heavy-duty powder-coated steel construction provide a durable basin for soaking and scrubbing small to mid-size parts without constant refilling.

The 2.64–3.17 GPM pump delivers steady solvent flow for cleaning carburetors, brackets, and wheel bearings, though it won't match the rinsing power of higher-flow models. The flow is sufficient for brush-on cleaning and soaking, making it a practical step up from a plastic basin for home shops that don't need commercial speed.

DIY mechanics who need a mid-size basin for petroleum-based parts cleaning and don't mind a quick hardware inspection upon unboxing will find this tank a solid value. The pump's operational hum is noticeable but fades into background noise in a detached garage; in a quiet attached shop it may be more present. The steel construction and powder coating hold up to routine use, though heavy commercial cycles will expose its limits.

However, the 0.5 GPM flow rate cannot break down heavy grease, and fluid can splash outside the basin during use. That limits it strictly to occasional cleaning of tiny parts. 💡 💡 Tip: Place a shallow tray under the basin to catch stray fluid — the pump's splash can splatter outside the basin.

buyingGuide.heading: Tank Capacity
buyingGuide.body: The total tank volume is often larger than the working solvent capacity you will actually use. A 20‑gallon washer might hold only 12 gallons of solvent once you account for the pump and parts tray. This determines how large a component you can fully submerge. For cleaning carburetors or wheel bearings, a 5‑gallon portable unit is enough, but to handle an engine block or cylinder head, you need at least a 20‑gallon basin. If you plan to scrub bigger castings regularly, a 40‑gallon deep tub prevents solvent overflow and gives you room to work.

buyingGuide.heading: Pump Flow Rate
buyingGuide.body: Flow rate, measured in gallons per minute (GPM), dictates how forcefully solvent hits your parts. A higher GPM means faster grime removal, but it can also cause splashing and mess outside the tank if the nozzle isn't adjustable. Models with 5+ GPM clean aggressively, which suits heavily soiled items. Lower flow around 0.5–1 GPM works for light dirt but struggles on baked‑on grease. The hidden trade-off: high flow can blast solvent past the basin lip and onto your floor. Adding a flow‑through brush or reducing pump speed helps contain the spray.

buyingGuide.heading: Solvent Compatibility
buyingGuide.body: This is the most critical spec that catches first‑time buyers. Many affordable washers, including popular Red Sun models, are designed exclusively for water‑based degreasers and will be destroyed by mineral spirits or other petroleum solvents. The pump seals and internal materials dissolve on contact, leading to leaks and pump failure in days. If you rely on mineral spirits for tough grease, you need a petroleum‑safe model — typically steel‑body units from JEGS or Eastwood. Always check the listing’s fine print: 'water‑based only' warnings are easily missed but non‑negotiable.

buyingGuide.heading: Build Material and Coating
buyingGuide.body: Most parts washers use steel with a powder‑coated finish for solvent resistance. The thickness of the steel and quality of the coating determine how well the tank handles years of chemical exposure and scraping. Cheaper coatings can peel when exposed to alkaline water‑based cleaners, leading to rust inside the basin. Look for an anti‑rust coating or heavy‑duty powder coat, especially if you will use harsh degreasers. Plastic models are lightweight and corrosion‑proof, but they lack the durability to support heavy parts and may crack.

buyingGuide.heading: Included Accessories
buyingGuide.body: A flow‑through brush lets you scrub while solvent flows directly through the bristles, targeting tight crevices without a separate soak. A removable parts basket simplifies draining and cleaning. These extras are nice, but they add to the price. The key trade-off: a washer with a brush and basket often has a slightly smaller working capacity because the basket takes up space. If you clean small, intricate components, a brush is worth the extra cost. For large batch cleaning, focus on raw tank volume first.

faq.question: Can I use mineral spirits in a water‑based parts washer?
faq.answer: No. Water‑based only washers use pump seals and gaskets that swell and degrade when exposed to petroleum solvents. Pouring in mineral spirits will ruin the pump, often within days, and void the warranty. If you prefer traditional solvent cleaning, choose a petroleum‑safe model like the JEGS 5‑Gallon or Eastwood 40‑Gallon.

faq.question: Why does my parts washer pump stop working after a few uses?
faq.answer: Most budget pumps have limited duty cycles and can burn out if run continuously without cooling. The pump may also fail from solvent incompatibility — water‑based cleaners can be too alkaline for some internal parts. Shipping damage is another culprit: a jarred motor can work initially then seize. Inspect the unit on arrival, and consider keeping a spare pump on hand.

faq.question: How do I stop my parts washer from leaking at the neck?
faq.answer: Leaks at the gooseneck base are common due to loose factory fittings. Tighten the lock nut manually and apply plumber’s tape to the threads for a better seal. For persistent drips, a thin bead of solvent‑resistant sealant around the neck plate can prevent fluid from wicking down the hose. Avoid over‑tightening, which cracks the basin.

faq.question: What size parts washer do I need for cleaning engine blocks?
faq.answer: An engine block needs a tub at least 30 inches long and deep enough to submerge the height. A 20‑gallon washer can handle smaller blocks like those from inline‑4 engines, but a 40‑gallon model with a deep tank (like the Eastwood) comfortably fits V8 blocks and cylinder heads. The extra capacity also reduces splashing when wrestling heavy castings.
